keyConcepts.label: Propositions and connectives
keyConcepts.microLesson: A proposition is a statement with a definite truth value: '$17$ is prime' qualifies, 'close the door' does not. Three connectives build compound statements: $p \land q$ (AND) is true only when both parts are true; $p \lor q$ (OR) is true when at least one is â€” inclusive, so 'both' counts; $\lnot p$ (NOT) flips the value. Evaluate inside-out like arithmetic, with $\lnot$ binding tightest. Example: with $p$ true, $q$ false, $r$ true, $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$ gives true $\land$ false, which is false.

keyConcepts.label: Truth tables
keyConcepts.microLesson: A truth table checks a formula on every possible input: $n$ variables give $2^n$ rows (two choices per variable, multiplied), listed for two variables in the standard order $(p, q) = $ TT, TF, FT, FF. Fill one column per subformula, working inside-out. A formula true in every row is a tautology â€” $p \lor \lnot p$ has column T, T â€” and one false in every row is a contradiction, like $p \land \lnot p$; everything else is contingent. Because the rows are finite, the table is a complete proof, a luxury most of mathematics never gets.

keyConcepts.label: Implication and contrapositive
keyConcepts.microLesson: $p \to q$ ('if $p$ then $q$') is false in exactly one case: $p$ true and $q$ false â€” a promise is broken only when the condition fired and the payoff didn't â€” so its column in TT, TF, FT, FF order is T, F, T, T. When $p$ is false the implication is vacuously true. The contrapositive $\lnot q \to \lnot p$ has the identical column, so it is logically equivalent to $p \to q$. The converse $q \to p$ is NOT: with $p$ false and $q$ true, $p \to q$ is true while $q \to p$ is false.

keyConcepts.label: De Morgan's laws and equivalence
keyConcepts.microLesson: Negation flips the connective as it passes inside: $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$ and $\lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$. In code, `!(x > 0 && y > 0)` becomes `x <= 0 || y <= 0`. Two formulas are logically equivalent exactly when their truth-table columns agree on all $2^n$ rows â€” check $\lnot(p \land q)$ against $\lnot p \lor \lnot q$: both read F, T, T, T. The classic error keeps the connective: $\lnot p \land \lnot q$ has column F, F, F, T, which is $\lnot(p \lor q)$, a different statement entirely.

lecture.body:
## The logic inside every `if`

Every `if` statement in every program ever written is propositional logic. `if (user.isLoggedIn && !user.isBanned)` is the formula $p \land \lnot q$ wearing a costume. The AND and OR gates etched into your CPU â€” billions of them, switching billions of times a second â€” are physical implementations of the connectives on this page. SQL `WHERE` clauses, search filters, firewall rules, spreadsheet formulas: all of it compiles down to the same tiny algebra of true and false. This is the rare topic where the math *is* the application, symbol for symbol.

## Propositions and the three basic connectives

A **proposition** is a statement that is definitively true or false: '$17$ is prime' (true), '$2 + 2 = 5$' (false), 'it is raining in Oslo right now' (one of the two, even if you don't know which). Questions, commands, and opinions don't qualify â€” 'close the door' has no truth value.

Compound statements are built from three connectives:

- **AND** ($p \land q$): true only when *both* parts are true.
- **OR** ($p \lor q$): true when *at least one* part is true â€” inclusive, so 'both' counts. (The either-or of everyday speech, exclusive or, is a different, derived operator.)
- **NOT** ($\lnot p$): flips the value.

Evaluation works inside-out, exactly like arithmetic, with $\lnot$ binding tightest. Take $p$ true, $q$ false, $r$ true and evaluate $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$: the parenthesis gives true, $\lnot r$ gives false, and true AND false is **false**. Two small operations, zero ambiguity, machine-checkable â€” that is the whole game.

## Truth tables: brute-force certainty

A formula with $n$ variables has only $2^n$ possible input combinations â€” two choices per variable, multiplied. Three variables? $2^3 = 8$ rows. Ten? 1024. Small enough to check *every single case*, which is a kind of total proof most of mathematics can only dream about.

A **truth table** lists each combination in a fixed order â€” conventionally $(p, q) = $ TT, TF, FT, FF â€” and computes the formula row by row, one column per subformula. For $(p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q)$ you would compute $p \lor q$, then $p \land q$, then its negation, then the final AND, getting F, T, T, F down the rows. (That formula is exclusive or: true when the inputs *differ*.)

Two special verdicts matter. A **tautology** is true in every row â€” $p \lor \lnot p$ cannot fail. A **contradiction** is false in every row â€” $p \land \lnot p$ cannot succeed. Everything else is *contingent*: its truth depends on the inputs.

## Implication, the connective everyone gets wrong

The conditional $p \to q$ ('if $p$ then $q$') is false in exactly one case: $p$ true and $q$ false. That's it. The full column, in TT, TF, FT, FF order, is T, F, T, T.

Why is false-implies-anything true? Read $p \to q$ as a promise: 'if it rains, I'll cancel the game.' If it doesn't rain, the promise is unbroken no matter what happens â€” canceling anyway doesn't make me a liar. A promise is only *broken* when the condition fired and the payoff didn't. Logicians call the false-$p$ rows **vacuously true**.

Three relatives of $p \to q$ have names worth memorizing:

- the **converse** $q \to p$ â€” *not* equivalent to the original;
- the **inverse** $\lnot p \to \lnot q$ â€” also not equivalent (it is the converse in disguise);
- the **contrapositive** $\lnot q \to \lnot p$ â€” **logically equivalent** ($\equiv$), same column in every row.

The contrapositive is a workhorse: proving 'if $n^2$ is even then $n$ is even' head-on is awkward, but its contrapositive 'if $n$ is odd then $n^2$ is odd' is one line of algebra. Confusing a statement with its converse, meanwhile, is *the* classic fallacy â€” 'this bug always crashes the app' does not mean 'every crash is that bug.'

## De Morgan's laws: how negation moves

Negation does not distribute politely; it flips the connective as it passes through:

$$\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q, \qquad \lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$$

'Not (rich and famous)' means 'not rich *or* not famous' â€” the everyday mistake is keeping the AND. Programmers live these laws daily: `!(x > 0 && y > 0)` rewrites to `x <= 0 || y <= 0`, which is usually the version a human can actually read. Applied repeatedly, De Morgan pushes a negation through any nesting: $\lnot(p \land (q \lor r)) \equiv \lnot p \lor (\lnot q \land \lnot r)$.

And how do you *know* a rewrite is safe? Truth tables again: two formulas are **logically equivalent** exactly when their columns match on every row. With $n$ variables that is a finite, mechanical check â€” which is why hardware verifiers can certify that an optimized circuit computes the same function as the reference design on *all* inputs before a chip is ever manufactured.

## Where you'll use it by Friday

Simplifying a gnarly conditional with De Morgan, reading a contract's 'unless' correctly, writing a search filter that returns what you actually meant, debugging why a guard clause never fires: propositional logic is the assembly language of clear thinking. Learn the truth tables cold â€” predicates, proofs, sets, and counting all stand on this floor.

application.summary: Propositional logic is the one topic you already run in production: every program conditional, logic gate, SQL filter, and formal-verification query is a compound proposition, and De Morgan plus truth-table equivalence is how you refactor them without changing behavior.
application.workedExample:
**Untangling a guard clause with De Morgan.**

You inherit this access check in a code review:

`if (!(user.isAdmin || (user.hasLicense && !user.isSuspended))) denyAccess();`

Let $a$ = 'user is admin', $l$ = 'user has a license', $s$ = 'user is suspended'. The condition is $\lnot(a \lor (l \land \lnot s))$ â€” a negation wrapped around the whole policy, which is exactly why nobody on the team can read it.

*Push the negation through the outer OR* (De Morgan, flipping $\lor$ to $\land$): $\lnot a \land \lnot(l \land \lnot s)$.

*Push it through the inner AND* (flipping $\land$ to $\lor$, and cancelling the double negation on $s$): $\lnot(l \land \lnot s) \equiv \lnot l \lor s$. The whole condition becomes $\lnot a \land (\lnot l \lor s)$:

`if (!user.isAdmin && (!user.hasLicense || user.isSuspended)) denyAccess();`

Read aloud, the rewrite finally states the policy: deny when the user is not an admin AND either has no license or is suspended.

*Trust, but verify.* Spot-check the row $a = F$, $l = T$, $s = T$ (a suspended, licensed non-admin). Original: $l \land \lnot s = T \land F = F$; then $a \lor F = F$; negated gives **true** â€” deny. Rewrite: $\lnot a = T$ and $\lnot l \lor s = F \lor T = T$; $T \land T$ is **true** â€” deny. Same verdict. Run all $2^3 = 8$ rows and the columns match everywhere, so the refactor is *provably* behavior-preserving, not just 'looks right.' That is the same move â€” scaled up to millions of gates and run by a SAT solver â€” that chip designers use to prove an optimized circuit equals its specification.
domainNotes: **Circuits:** AND, OR, and NOT gates are propositional connectives in silicon; every truth table you write is a component specification, and full-table equivalence checking is how an optimized chip design is proven identical to the reference.
**Programming:** `&&`, `||`, `!` short-circuit â€” `p && q` never evaluates `q` when `p` is false â€” which is why `x != 0 && y / x > 1` is safe and why swapping its operands is a bug.
**Databases and search:** an SQL `WHERE NOT (a OR b)` and a search query like `-(cats OR dogs)` obey De Morgan; negate a compound filter wrong and the query silently returns the wrong rows.

**Formal verification:** SAT solvers decide the satisfiability of propositional formulas with millions of variables; they power the model checkers that have caught real bugs in CPU designs and flight software before shipping.
**Law and contracts:** 'unless', 'except when', and nested 'and/or' clauses are compound propositions; a drafting error in a negation is a De Morgan error with money attached.

problems.prompt: Let $p$ be true and $q$ be false. Evaluate each compound statement. (Type 'true' or 'false' for each step.)

steps.instruction: Evaluate $p \land q$. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: AND needs *both* parts true; $q$ is false.

steps.instruction: Evaluate $p \lor q$. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: OR needs at least one true part; $p$ is true.

steps.instruction: Evaluate $\lnot p$. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: NOT flips the value of $p$.

problems.solutionNarrative: With $p = T$, $q = F$: $p \land q$ fails (both needed), $p \lor q$ holds (one suffices), and $\lnot p$ flips true to false.

problems.prompt: Your code contains `if (x > 3 && x < 10)`. In logic this is $p \land q$ with $p$: '$x > 3$' and $q$: '$x < 10$'. Suppose $x = 12$.

steps.instruction: Is $p$ ($x > 3$) true or false? Type 'true' or 'false'.
Is $q$ ($x < 10$) true or false?
steps.hint: $12$ is not below $10$.

steps.instruction: Does the `if` body run â€” is $p \land q$ true or false?
steps.hint: AND needs both; one conjunct failed.

problems.solutionNarrative: At $x = 12$: $p$ is true, $q$ is false, so $p \land q$ is false and the branch is skipped â€” an `if` condition is just a proposition evaluated at runtime.

problems.prompt: Let $p$ be true, $q$ be false, and $r$ be true. Evaluate $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$ inside-out.

steps.instruction: Evaluate the parenthesis $p \lor q$ first. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $p$ alone is enough for an OR.

steps.instruction: Evaluate $\lnot r$.
steps.hint: $r$ is true; NOT flips it.

steps.instruction: Combine: $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$ is true AND false. Final value?
steps.hint: AND with a false operand is false.

problems.solutionNarrative: $p \lor q = T$, $\lnot r = F$, and $T \land F = F$. Evaluate compound statements inside-out, exactly like arithmetic with parentheses.

problems.prompt: A program runs `if (a != 0 && b / a > 2)` with $a = 0$ and $b = 10$. Like most languages, this one short-circuits: in $p \land q$, if $p$ is false it never evaluates $q$ at all.

steps.instruction: Evaluate the first operand $p$: is $a \ne 0$ true or false?
steps.hint: $a$ is exactly $0$.

steps.instruction: Given short-circuiting, does the program evaluate the second operand $b / a > 2$ at all? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: False AND anything is already false, so evaluation stops.

steps.instruction: What is the value of the whole condition? Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $F \land q$ is false for every $q$.

steps.instruction: If the language evaluated BOTH operands eagerly instead, would this line divide by zero? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: $b / a$ with $a = 0$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $a \ne 0$ is false, so short-circuit AND stops immediately: the condition is false and $b/a$ is never computed. The operand order is load-bearing â€” the logic ($F \land q \equiv F$) is exactly what makes the guard safe.

problems.prompt: A truth table needs one row per combination of truth values, and each of the $n$ variables independently takes 2 values â€” so there are $2^n$ rows.

steps.instruction: How many rows does a truth table with 3 variables have? (Give a number.)
How many rows for 5 variables?
How many rows for 10 variables?
steps.hint: $2^{10}$ â€” the famous 'about a thousand'.

problems.solutionNarrative: Rows double with each extra variable: $2^3 = 8$, $2^5 = 32$, $2^{10} = 1024$. Exponential growth is why brute-force table checking works for small $n$ and needs SAT solvers for large $n$.

problems.prompt: Build truth-table columns for $p \land q$ and $p \lor q$, with rows in the standard order $(p, q) = $ TT, TF, FT, FF.

steps.instruction: Give the column for $p \land q$. Use 1 for true, 0 for false, as an ordered comma list, e.g. 1,0,1,1.
steps.hint: AND is true only in the row where both inputs are true.

steps.instruction: Give the column for $p \lor q$ in the same format.
steps.hint: OR fails only when both inputs are false.

steps.instruction: In how many of the 4 rows do the two columns disagree? (Give a number.)
steps.hint: They differ exactly where the inputs differ: TF and FT.

problems.solutionNarrative: $p \land q$: 1,0,0,0 and $p \lor q$: 1,1,1,0 â€” they disagree in the two mixed rows TF and FT, where OR holds but AND fails.

problems.prompt: Build the full truth table for $(p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q)$ â€” 'one or the other but not both' â€” with rows in the order $(p, q) = $ TT, TF, FT, FF, and classify the formula.

steps.instruction: Row TT: $p \lor q$ is true and $p \land q$ is true, so $\lnot(p \land q)$ is false. What is the formula's value in this row? Use 1 for true, 0 for false.
steps.hint: $T \land F = F$, so write 0.

steps.instruction: Give the formula's full column for rows TT, TF, FT, FF. Use 1 for true, 0 for false, as an ordered comma list, e.g. 1,1,0,0.
steps.hint: In the mixed rows, $p \lor q$ is true and $p \land q$ is false; in FF both fail.

steps.instruction: Classify: is this formula a tautology, a contradiction, or neither? Type exactly one word: 'tautology', 'contradiction', or 'neither'.
steps.hint: The column contains both 1s and 0s.

problems.solutionNarrative: The column is 0,1,1,0 â€” true exactly when the inputs differ (this formula is exclusive or). Since it is sometimes true and sometimes false, it is contingent: neither a tautology nor a contradiction.

problems.prompt: Evaluate $p \lor \lnot p$ ('$p$ or not $p$') in both possible rows and classify it.

steps.instruction: When $p$ is true: $p \lor \lnot p$ is true or false? Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: The left disjunct already holds.

steps.instruction: When $p$ is false: true or false?
steps.hint: Now $\lnot p$ holds instead.

steps.instruction: True in every row â€” what is such a formula called? Type one word: 'tautology' or 'contradiction'.
steps.hint: A contradiction would be false in every row.

problems.solutionNarrative: $p \lor \lnot p$ is true whichever value $p$ takes â€” the law of the excluded middle, the simplest tautology. Its mirror image $p \land \lnot p$ is the simplest contradiction.

problems.prompt: The implication $p \to q$ is false in exactly one case: $p$ true and $q$ false. Evaluate it for each pair of values. (Type 'true' or 'false' for each step.)

steps.instruction: $p$ true, $q$ false: what is $p \to q$?
steps.hint: This is the one broken-promise row.

steps.instruction: $p$ false, $q$ true: what is $p \to q$?
steps.hint: The condition never fired, so the promise stands â€” vacuously true.

steps.instruction: $p$ false, $q$ false: what is $p \to q$?
steps.hint: Again vacuous: false $p$ makes the implication true automatically.

problems.solutionNarrative: Only $T \to F$ is false; both false-$p$ rows are vacuously true. The full column in TT, TF, FT, FF order is T, F, T, T.

problems.prompt: A coach promises: 'If it rains ($p$), the game is cancelled ($q$).' Saturday it does NOT rain, but the coach cancels the game anyway.

steps.instruction: Here $p$ is false and $q$ is true. Is the promise $p \to q$ true or false in this scenario? Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: The promise only constrains what happens WHEN it rains.

steps.instruction: The promise is broken in exactly one scenario. In that scenario, is $p$ true or false?
steps.hint: It must actually rain for the promise to be tested.

steps.instruction: And in that broken-promise scenario, is $q$ true or false?
steps.hint: Rain, yet the game goes ahead.

problems.solutionNarrative: With no rain, the promise is vacuously true regardless of the cancellation: $F \to T = T$. The only falsifying scenario is rain and no cancellation â€” $p$ true, $q$ false.

problems.prompt: Prove by truth table that $p \to q$ and $\lnot q \to \lnot p$ are logically equivalent. Use rows in the order $(p, q) = $ TT, TF, FT, FF.

steps.instruction: Give the column for $p \to q$. Use 1 for true, 0 for false, as an ordered comma list, e.g. 0,1,0,1.
steps.hint: False only in the row TF, where $p$ is true and $q$ is false.

steps.instruction: Now the column for $\lnot q \to \lnot p$, same format. (In row TF: $\lnot q$ is true, $\lnot p$ is false.)
steps.hint: It fails exactly when $\lnot q$ is true and $\lnot p$ is false â€” i.e. $p$ true, $q$ false.

steps.instruction: $\lnot q \to \lnot p$ is called the ___ of $p \to q$. Type one word: 'converse', 'inverse', or 'contrapositive'.
steps.hint: Swap AND negate both sides.

steps.instruction: The two columns match in every row. Are the formulas logically equivalent? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Matching columns on all $2^n$ rows is the definition of equivalence.

problems.solutionNarrative: Both columns are 1,0,1,1 â€” each formula fails only in the row $p = T$, $q = F$ â€” so $p \to q \equiv \lnot q \to \lnot p$. This is why proving the contrapositive proves the original statement.

problems.prompt: Start from the statement: 'If $n$ is even, then $n^2$ is even' â€” an implication $p \to q$.

steps.instruction: 'If $n^2$ is even, then $n$ is even' ($q \to p$) is the ___ of the original. Type one word: 'converse' or 'contrapositive'.
steps.hint: The two sides are swapped but nothing is negated.

steps.instruction: 'If $n^2$ is odd, then $n$ is odd' ($\lnot q \to \lnot p$) is the ___. Type 'converse' or 'contrapositive'.
steps.hint: Swapped AND both sides negated ('odd' = 'not even').

steps.instruction: Which of the two is guaranteed logically equivalent to the original? Type 'converse' or 'contrapositive'.
steps.hint: $p \to q \equiv \lnot q \to \lnot p$; the converse is independent.

problems.solutionNarrative: Swapping alone gives the converse; swapping and negating gives the contrapositive, the only relative guaranteed equivalent to $p \to q$. (Here the converse happens to be true too â€” but that needs its own proof.)

problems.prompt: De Morgan's law says $\lnot(p \lor q)$ equals $\lnot p$ combined with $\lnot q$ â€” but negation flips the connective on the way in.

steps.instruction: Which connective joins $\lnot p$ and $\lnot q$ in the equivalent form of $\lnot(p \lor q)$? Type one word: 'and' or 'or'.
steps.hint: 'Neither p nor q' means both fail: NOT flips OR to AND.

steps.instruction: Evaluate $\lnot(p \lor q)$ at $p$ true, $q$ false. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $p \lor q$ is true, then negate.

steps.instruction: Now evaluate $\lnot p \land \lnot q$ at the same values. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $\lnot p$ is already false, so the AND fails â€” same verdict, as the law promises.

problems.solutionNarrative: $\lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$: negation turns OR into AND. At $p = T$, $q = F$ both forms evaluate to false, as equivalence demands.

problems.prompt: A code review flags the condition `!(x > 0 && y > 0)`. Test it with $x = 3$, $y = -2$, then rewrite it with De Morgan.

steps.instruction: Evaluate the inner conjunction $x > 0 \land y > 0$ at $x = 3$, $y = -2$. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $y > 0$ fails.

steps.instruction: So what does the whole negated condition evaluate to? Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: NOT of false.

steps.instruction: De Morgan rewrites `!(a && b)` as `!a` ? `!b`. Which connective? Type one word: 'and' or 'or'.
steps.hint: Negation flips AND to OR.

steps.instruction: Check the rewrite: evaluate $\lnot(x > 0) \lor \lnot(y > 0)$ at the same values. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $\lnot(y > 0)$ is true, and one true disjunct suffices.

problems.solutionNarrative: The inner AND is false, so its negation is true. De Morgan gives the readable equivalent `!(x > 0) || !(y > 0)`, i.e. `x <= 0 || y <= 0`, which evaluates to the same value â€” true â€” at $(3, -2)$.

problems.prompt: Verify De Morgan's first law, $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$, by full truth table â€” and expose the classic wrong answer. Rows in the order $(p, q) = $ TT, TF, FT, FF.

steps.instruction: Give the column for $\lnot(p \land q)$. Use 1 for true, 0 for false, as an ordered comma list, e.g. 1,0,1,0.
steps.hint: $p \land q$ is 1,0,0,0 â€” now negate every entry.

steps.instruction: Give the column for $\lnot p \lor \lnot q$, same format.
steps.hint: It fails only when both negations fail â€” the row TT.

steps.instruction: Do the columns match in every row â€” are the formulas equivalent? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Compare entry by entry.

steps.instruction: The classic mistake keeps the connective: $\lnot p \land \lnot q$ has column 0,0,0,1. Does IT match $\lnot(p \land q)$? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: They already disagree in row TF.

problems.solutionNarrative: Both $\lnot(p \land q)$ and $\lnot p \lor \lnot q$ have column 0,1,1,1, so they are equivalent. The tempting $\lnot p \land \lnot q$ has column 0,0,0,1 â€” that is $\lnot(p \lor q)$, the *other* De Morgan law.

problems.prompt: An access check reads `if (!(p && (q || r)))` where $p$: 'user is an admin', $q$: 'file is public', $r$: 'user owns the file'. De Morgan (applied twice) rewrites it as $\lnot p \lor (\lnot q \land \lnot r)$. Evaluate both forms for an admin looking at a private file they don't own: $p$ true, $q$ false, $r$ false.

steps.instruction: Evaluate the inner disjunction $q \lor r$ at $q$ false, $r$ false. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: Both disjuncts fail.

steps.instruction: Evaluate $p \land (q \lor r)$. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: True AND false.

steps.instruction: So the original condition $\lnot(p \land (q \lor r))$ is...? Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: Negate your last answer.

steps.instruction: Check the De Morgan rewrite: evaluate $\lnot p \lor (\lnot q \land \lnot r)$ at the same values. Type 'true' or 'false'.
steps.hint: $\lnot p$ is false, but $\lnot q \land \lnot r$ is $T \land T$.

problems.solutionNarrative: $q \lor r = F$, so $p \land (q \lor r) = F$ and its negation is true. The rewritten form gives $F \lor (T \land T) = T$ â€” the same verdict, because pushing $\lnot$ through flips $\land$ to $\lor$ and $\lor$ to $\land$ at each level.
